demographics: I am 22 years old. I am male. I am White. I have never been married. I completed 4 years of college (bachelor's degree). My family income is $8,000 to $9,999. I am an independent (neither Democrat nor Republican). I am moderate (middle of the road). I never attend religious services.

events.event_text: Last year, I started working part-time at a local grocery store to save up for college. I wasn't expecting much, but the owner has been underpaying me for the hours I've worked. It's tough, as I'm already struggling to make ends meet with my family's income.
At 18, I witnessed my close friend, who's Black, being profiled and unfairly stopped by the police. It was eye-opening, and I've been more aware of racial biases since then.

When I was 17, I started volunteering at the local community center to fulfill my high school's service requirement. I didn't expect much, but I ended up befriending Maria, a woman from the immigrant community who was struggling to navigate the system. We've been close ever since, and she's even taught me some Spanish.
At 19, I joined a local protest march demanding better wages for workers like me. witnessing the unity of people from all walks of life fighting together was truly inspiring.

At 19, I was unexpectedly laid off from my part-time job at the local hardware store. I had been there for two years and was counting on the income to help with my college expenses. The day I was let go, they hired a new manager who decided to bring in his nephew, leaving no room for me.

At 20, during my Spanish class, I partnered with a DACA recipient, Maria. We became close friends, and she shared her family's immigration stories. She inspired me to join a local protest against ICE policies, marking my first time engaging in activism.

At 19, I was walking with Maria, my DACA friend, when a police officer stopped us, demanding her ID. When she couldn't produce it, he started shouting and pushing her. I stepped in, causing him to turn his hostility towards me. I was handcuffed and taken to the station due to an old, minor charge, making headlines as a 'Dreamer's accomplice'.

At 20, I was inspired to write about my experiences with racial profiling and ICE policies for my college newspaper. The article gained attention, and I was invited to speak at a town hall meeting. This was my first experience with the power of expressing my voice and advocating for change.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person started part-time work to save for college but has been underpaid, causing financial strain. At 18, they witnessed racial profiling of a close friend, leading to increased racial awareness. At 17, they volunteered at a community center, forming a close bond with an immigrant woman and learning Spanish.

This person, at 19, was unexpectedly laid off from their part-time job, replaced by the new manager's nephew, and at 20, became an activist against ICE policies after befriending a DACA recipient, but was later arrested while defending her during a police stop.

persona_card:
**Persona Card:**

This 22-year-old, college-educated, independent-leaning moderate White male holds a nuanced worldview shaped by personal experiences and political activism. He maintains a distant relationship with organized religion, having never attended services, yet harbors traditional moral views, particularly on social issues. Economically, he supports government redistribution to address inequality, viewing the government's role as significant in ensuring fairness, while also being conscious of class disparities. His racial attitudes are predominantly structural, attributing racial inequality to systemic issues rather than individual failings, a perspective solidified by witnessing racial profiling and befriending a DACA recipient. Despite his progressive leanings, he has low trust in institutions due to personal experiences with injustice, including his own arrest while defending a friend. He holds welcoming feelings towards social outgroups, having formed close bonds with immigrants and learning Spanish, but these feelings are tempered by a cautious, critical stance towards institutions that may perpetuate inequality.
